problem: Let h(n) count the number of incongruent sets of n points in ℝ^2 which minimise the diameter subject to the constraint that d(x,y)≥ 1 for all points x≠ y. Is it true that h(n)→ ∞?
original_latex: Let $h(n)$ count the number of incongruent sets of $n$ points in $\mathbb{R}^2$ which minimise the diameter subject to the constraint that $d(x,y)\geq 1$ for all points $x\neq y$. Is it true that $h(n)\to \infty$?
additional_context:
It is not even known whether h(n)≥ 2 for all large n.

See also [99].
reference_proof_hint:
This is not known.

It is an open problem of Erdős (listed as Erdős Problem #103). In fact, even the much weaker statement “for all large $n$, there are **at least two** non-congruent diameter-minimising configurations” is not proved. ([erdosproblems.com][1])

For context: for large $n$, the **shape** of a diameter-minimising set is expected to look like a dense “triangular/hexagonal lattice” packing inside something close to a disk (because the triangular lattice is the densest packing pattern in the plane, and this gives the right asymptotic behaviour). But turning that into a proof about **exact** minimisers for each $n$, and then counting how many different minimisers exist, is exactly what is hard here. ([erdosproblems.com][2])

[1]: https://www.erdosproblems.com/forum/thread/103 "

    Erdős Problem #103 - Discussion thread

"
[2]: https://www.erdosproblems.com/99 "
